summary: Gruber analyzes Bloomberg's report that Apple is preparing to allow third-party app stores on iOS to comply with the EU's Digital Markets Act. He argues that Apple will still find ways to collect commissions on digital purchases regardless of sideloading, drawing parallels to Apple's Dutch dating app compliance. He dissects the DMA's actual language, finding it ambiguous on key questions like whether sideloading and third-party stores are both required, and who would qualify to run alternative stores. He predicts Apple will impose a byzantine approval process that makes third-party stores as unappealing as legally possible, and concludes the DMA is poorly written legislation crafted by people who lack technical understanding.
key_points: Apple will likely still demand commissions on digital purchases even through third-party stores, as Tim Cook views the commission as a fee for being on the iOS platform, not just for the App Store itself

The DMA's language is ambiguous — it uses 'or' between sideloading and third-party app stores, potentially allowing Apple to support only one, and its security exception clauses invite years of legal disputes

Riley Testut's argument highlights the perverse incentive: third-party app stores would compete by poaching existing App Store apps, forcing users to install multiple stores just to keep using current apps
Apple's compliance will likely involve a verification process with fees and contractual obligations so onerous that few companies could qualify to run alternative stores
The DMA is poorly drafted regulation — written by people without technical expertise who are dictating technical architecture
money_quotes: That last sentence surely strikes many of you as nutty.

Apple is going to want a commission. Tim Cook's testimony in the Epic lawsuit revealed that Apple doesn't see its App Store commissions as a fee for the App Store itself, but for being on the iOS platform at all.
Of course Apple is going to comply with E.U. laws, whether it agrees with those laws or not.

Anyone who is hoping that the DMA is going to force Apple to allow any and all third-party software you can imagine — more or less requiring Apple to treat iOS like it does MacOS — is, I think, setting themselves up for disappointment.
I foresee a byzantine approval system imposed by Apple even if Apple comes into it with nothing but the best intentions.
I somehow doubt that Apple's attitude would be 'let's make third-party app stores as appealing and useful as possible'.

The DMA's third-party app store mandate is not only unclear, it's like house-building regulations written by people who have no experience with architecture or carpentry — and who apparently don't think that architecture or carpentry expertise are necessary for dictating how houses should be built.
After more than a day of digging through the DMA, I conclude mostly that while it might be well intentioned, it's horribly written.
key_insight: The EU's Digital Markets Act is so ambiguously written that Apple will almost certainly find ways to comply with the letter of the law while preserving its commission structure and making third-party app stores as unappealing as legally possible.
